name: API lifecycle & security
description: API lifecycle and security from the Dennemeyer 100: contracts and data exchange, versioning/compatibility/deprecation, OAuth/OIDC/JWT, RBAC vs ABAC, API security essentials, and integration styles (webhooks, pagination, gRPC, sync vs async).

topics.name: API versioning & compatibility
topics.description: Evolving APIs without breaking consumers: additive changes, versioning strategies, deprecation.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

API versioning is how you change an API without breaking the programs already using it. The version can live in the URL (/v1/renewals, /v2/renewals), in a header, or in the media type.

## What problem does it solve?

Once external clients integrate against your API, any change can break them — and you usually cannot force them all to update at once. Versioning lets old clients keep working while new ones get the improvements.

## How it works

The compatibility rules matter more than where the version number lives:
- ADDING an optional field is safe — old clients simply ignore it.
- DELETING or RENAMING a field is a breaking change: a client that reads status breaks the day it becomes state.

Backward compatibility in practice: support both forms during a transition. If a field must be renamed, ship both fields, migrate consumers, and remove the old one only later.

Deprecation is a managed process, not a switch-off: the old endpoint or field keeps working but is documented as deprecated, with a migration guide and a clear deadline (e.g. six months). For external customers, a silently killed integration is the worst possible experience — communication is part of the engineering.

## Common mistakes

- Renaming a field and calling it a minor change — clients break
- Killing deprecated endpoints without a migration guide and deadline
- Versioning everything preemptively instead of designing additive changes

## Key terms

- Breaking change — anything that makes an existing client fail
- Additive change — new optional fields; the safe kind
- Deprecation — "still works, but scheduled to go away", with a date
- Migration guide — how to move from the old to the new

topics.name: RBAC vs ABAC
topics.description: Role-based vs attribute-based access control models and when each fits.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Two models for deciding what a logged-in user may do: RBAC (role-based access control — permissions attached to roles) and ABAC (attribute-based access control — permissions computed from properties of the user, the resource and the context).

## What problem does it solve?

Login only proves who someone is. Every action afterwards still needs the question "may THIS user do THIS to THIS data?" answered — and hardcoding those decisions everywhere becomes unmaintainable fast.

## How it works

First the parent distinction: authentication = who are you; authorization = what may you do. They are separate steps, and the second one runs on every data access — a user asking for customer 456's data with a token that says customer 123 must be refused, whatever the request claims.

RBAC: define roles (Admin, Support Agent, Finance User, Read-only), attach permissions to roles, assign roles to users. Simple to explain, easy to audit ("who can delete invoices? everyone with Finance-Admin"). Its weakness: exceptions. When you need "Support Agents may edit, but only in their own region, except for VIP accounts", roles multiply into a mess.

ABAC: decisions are rules over attributes — user.companyId == document.companyId AND document.sensitivity != "restricted". Far more expressive; the cost is that rules are harder to test, debug and audit than a role list.

The practical answer interviewers want: start with RBAC for clarity; add attribute-based conditions where real rules genuinely need them. And always apply least privilege: every user, service and process gets the MINIMUM access it needs — a reporting service that only reads should not be able to delete.

## Common mistakes

- Authorization checked at login only, not per request
- Role explosion: a new role for every exception instead of attribute rules
- Services running with admin-everything instead of least privilege

## Key terms

- Role — a named bundle of permissions (RBAC)
- Attribute — a property of user/resource/context that rules test (ABAC)
- Least privilege — minimum necessary access, for services too
- Authorization check — runs per request, not just at login

topics.name: API Contracts & Data Exchange
topics.description: The API as a contract; DTOs, canonical data models, mapping, and validation vs business rules.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Treating an API as a CONTRACT — a stable, documented agreement that clients build on — and the modelling layers (DTOs, canonical models, mapping) that keep the contract stable while your internals change.

## What problem does it solve?

External customers integrate against your API's exact shapes. If your internal database structure leaks into the API, every internal refactor becomes a breaking change for customers.

## How it works

A good contract covers more than endpoints: stable request/response shapes, validation rules, consistent error codes, pagination, versioning, auth, and documentation. OpenAPI/Swagger makes it machine-readable — partners integrate from the spec, seeing request bodies, response examples and error codes without ever seeing your code.

The layers that isolate the contract from your internals:
- DTO (Data Transfer Object): the object that crosses the API is NOT your database entity. The DTO exposes customerId and trademarkId; the entity's internal notes and audit columns stay inside. Your internals can now change freely.
- Domain model vs database model: a "Renewal" as a business concept (with status rules) may be stored across several tables. Different concerns, different shapes.
- Canonical data model: when many partners send the same information in different formats, convert everything at the edge into ONE internal model. The core system never learns each partner's quirks.
- Data mapping: the conversion layer handling date formats, enums, country codes ("DE" vs "Germany" → ISO code), currencies. Keep it explicit, tested and versioned.

One more separation: validation vs business rule. "The date field is malformed" is a validation error (HTTP 400). "This trademark cannot be renewed because it expired" is a business rule refusal (409/422 with a domain error). Different failures, different handling, different messages.

## Working with the backend team

Clarify the contract EARLY, before the frontend builds on assumptions: request/response shapes and the error body format; which UI states the data implies (loading, empty, error); the pagination model (offset or cursor); auth semantics — what returns 401 (not logged in) vs 403 (not allowed) and what the UI should do for each; and backward compatibility during changes. Most "frontend bugs" around data are really contract ambiguities discovered too late.

## Common mistakes

- Returning database entities directly — internals leak into the contract
- Validation and business-rule failures mixed into one generic 400
- Renaming/removing a field without a deprecation window
- Partner-specific field names leaking past the mapping layer into the core

## Key terms

- DTO — the API-facing object, decoupled from the DB entity
- Canonical model — one internal format all partner data converts to
- OpenAPI/Swagger — the machine-readable contract document
- Validation vs business rule — malformed input vs "not allowed" (400 vs 409/422)
- 401 vs 403 — unauthenticated vs unauthorized; the UI reacts differently

topics.name: API Security Essentials
topics.description: Beyond login: input validation, secrets, encryption in transit/at rest, audit logging.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Everything API security means BEYOND login: validating input, protecting secrets, encrypting data, and keeping an audit trail.

## What problem does it solve?

An API with perfect authentication can still be wide open: trusting client-side validation, leaking secrets in Git, sending tokens over plain HTTP, or having no record of who changed what.

## How it works

The anchor rule: the backend validates EVERYTHING, because the API is directly callable — the frontend's checks can be bypassed with a curl command. Validation covers types, required fields, lengths, enums — AND data-level authorization: the user asked for customer 456's portfolio, but their token says customer 123 → refuse, regardless of the request.

Secrets — database passwords, API keys, JWT signing keys, OAuth client secrets — never belong in Git or in logs. They live in a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Kubernetes Secrets) and are injected at runtime.

Encryption has two independent layers:
- In transit: TLS/HTTPS on every connection — browser to API AND service to service — so tokens and data cannot be read off the network.
- At rest: databases, disks, backups and object storage encrypted, so storage-level access does not yield readable data.
Neither layer replaces authorization; they are additional walls.

Audit logging is different from debug logging: it records WHO did WHAT — who changed the trademark status, when, from what value to what value, under which request id. For legal and payment data this is a compliance requirement.

## Common mistakes

- Trusting frontend validation — the API is directly callable
- Checking WHO the user is but not WHOSE data they asked for
- Secrets in Git or in logs
- Debug logging where an audit trail is legally required

## Key terms

- Backend validation — the only validation that counts
- Secrets manager — runtime injection instead of hardcoded credentials
- Encryption in transit / at rest — TLS on the wire / encrypted storage
- Audit log — the who-did-what record, kept separate from debug logs

topics.name: Webhooks vs Polling
topics.description: Who initiates the update — the client asking repeatedly, or your system calling out on events.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The two ways a partner system learns about changes in yours: polling — they ask repeatedly ("anything new?") — and webhooks — YOUR system calls a URL they registered, when an event happens.

## What problem does it solve?

A partner needs to know when a renewal's status changes. Polling every minute wastes requests (and is still up to a minute late); a webhook tells them the moment it happens.

## How it works

Polling: trivial to build, works everywhere, wasteful at frequency. Long polling (the server holds the request open until there is news) is the halfway house. Fine for low-frequency needs.

Webhooks are efficient push — but production webhooks are a real subsystem you now own:

- Retries with backoff: their endpoint WILL be down sometimes.
- Signing the payload (HMAC) so the receiver can verify the call really came from you.
- Idempotency: they may receive the same event twice; include an event id.
- Timeouts on the delivery call, and a delivery log so support can answer "did you send it?".

The rule of thumb: consume webhooks when offered, offer webhooks when partners integrate with you — and keep a polling endpoint as the fallback for partners who cannot receive calls.

Related choices live in their own topics: pagination strategies (how large lists transfer), sync vs async communication (does the caller wait), and gRPC (the service-to-service protocol option).

## Common mistakes

- Webhooks shipped without retries, signatures or delivery logs
- No event id in the payload — receivers cannot dedupe
- Polling every second "temporarily" — forever

## Key terms

- Webhook — event push to a URL the receiver registered
- Long polling — the request waits until there is news
- HMAC signature — proof the webhook call is authentic
- Delivery log — the record of what was sent, when, and the response

topics.name: Pagination Strategies
topics.description: Offset vs cursor — transferring large lists without duplicates or gaps.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Pagination breaks a large list into pages the client fetches one at a time. The two main schemes: offset ("skip 5000, give me 50") and cursor ("give me 50 after this marker").

## What problem does it solve?

Returning a million rows in one response is impossible for everyone involved. But naive paging has a subtle correctness problem: lists CHANGE while being paged, and offset paging then shows duplicates or silently skips rows.

## How it works

Offset pagination (?offset=5000&limit=50): simple, supports "jump to page 100", trivial in SQL. Two weaknesses: on a list that changes between requests, inserting or deleting rows shifts everything — page 3 shows a row you already saw on page 2 (duplicate) or skips one (gap); and deep offsets are slow — the database must count past all 5000 skipped rows.

Cursor pagination (?cursor=abc&limit=50): the cursor is an opaque marker encoding "after this row" (typically the sort key of the last returned row). Stable on changing data — inserts and deletes cannot shift what "after row X" means — and equally fast at any depth. The costs: no random page jumps, and the sort order must be stable and unique (id as a tiebreaker).

The rule of thumb: internal admin tables with page numbers → offset is fine. External APIs and infinite scrolling over live data → cursor.

## Common mistakes

- Offset paging on fast-changing lists — duplicates and gaps
- Deep offsets scanning past millions of rows
- Cursors built on a non-unique sort column — unstable ordering
- Promising page numbers with cursors — they cannot do random jumps

## Key terms

- Offset/limit — skip N rows; simple but shifts on changing data
- Cursor — opaque "after this row" marker; stable and fast
- Stable sort key — unique ordering the cursor relies on

topics.name: Sync vs Async Communication
topics.description: Does the caller wait? Deciding by business dependency, not preference.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The choice between synchronous communication — the caller sends a request and WAITS for the answer — and asynchronous — the caller hands the work off (usually to a queue) and moves on, answering "accepted, in progress".

## What problem does it solve?

A synchronous call chains the caller's fate to the callee's: if document generation takes 30 seconds, the API request waits 30 seconds, its connection and thread held the whole time. Slow or flaky downstreams make every caller slow and flaky too.

## How it works

Synchronous: simple to build and reason about — the answer is right there. Right when the caller genuinely cannot proceed without the result: validating credentials, a fraud check that must PASS before confirming.

Asynchronous: validate the request, enqueue the heavy work, answer 202 Accepted with a pending status. The caller is decoupled from the slow part; the queue absorbs spikes; the worker retries failures without the user waiting. The UI shows "processing" honestly and updates when done (polling the status or receiving a push).

The decision rule: what does the USER have to wait for? Only that stays synchronous. Everything else — notifications, document generation, syncing other systems — is not on the critical path and belongs behind a queue. Sync vs async is a business-dependency question, not a style preference.

## Common mistakes

- Blocking the request on slow work instead of queueing and answering 202
- Making a gating step (fraud check) async and confirming before it passed
- Async everywhere — even the reads the user is actively waiting on
- No status endpoint/notification — "accepted" with no way to learn the outcome

## Key terms

- 202 Accepted — "queued, in progress" — the async answer
- Critical path — only what the user must wait for stays sync
- Pending status — the honest UI for in-flight async work

questions.title: Evolving an API without breaking clients
questions.prompt: Your public API needs a breaking change, but hundreds of clients depend on it. How do you handle API evolution?
questions.answerStructureHint: Prefer additive/backward-compatible changes; when truly breaking: version, run both, deprecation policy with communication and migration window.

expectedPoints.label: Prefers backward-compatible changes
expectedPoints.description: Add fields/endpoints instead of changing or removing; tolerant readers ignore unknown fields.

expectedPoints.label: Versions and runs both
expectedPoints.description: Introduces v2 (URL/header), keeps v1 running in parallel during migration.

expectedPoints.label: Deprecation policy with communication
expectedPoints.description: Announce, document, set a sunset date, monitor v1 usage before turning it off.

followUps.prompt: How does the same problem look for event schemas on a message queue?

questions.title: OAuth2, OIDC and JWT
questions.prompt: Explain the relationship between OAuth2, OpenID Connect and JWT. What does each one actually do?
questions.answerStructureHint: OAuth2 = delegated authorization (access tokens), OIDC = identity layer on top (ID token), JWT = self-contained signed token format.

expectedPoints.label: OAuth2 = delegated authorization
expectedPoints.description: Lets an app access resources on a user's behalf via access tokens — authorization, not login.

expectedPoints.label: OIDC adds identity
expectedPoints.description: OpenID Connect layers login/identity on OAuth2 via the ID token and userinfo.

expectedPoints.label: JWT = signed, self-contained token
expectedPoints.description: Signed claims readable without a DB lookup; validate signature and expiry; revocation is the hard part.

followUps.prompt: Where would you store tokens in a browser app, and what are the risks of each option?

questions.title: RBAC vs ABAC
questions.prompt: Compare role-based and attribute-based access control. When does RBAC stop being enough?
questions.answerStructureHint: RBAC: permissions via roles — simple, auditable. ABAC: policies over attributes (user, resource, context) — fine-grained. Role explosion is the tipping point.

expectedPoints.label: RBAC basics and strengths
expectedPoints.description: Users get roles, roles carry permissions; simple to reason about and audit.

expectedPoints.label: ABAC: policies over attributes
expectedPoints.description: Decisions from attributes of user, resource and context (owner, department, time, tenant).

expectedPoints.label: Knows the tipping point
expectedPoints.description: Role explosion — combinatorial roles per department/region/tenant — signals the need for ABAC; ABAC costs complexity and testability.

followUps.prompt: How does the principle of least privilege apply to whichever model you pick?

questions.title: Designing an API as a contract
questions.prompt: You are exposing an API to external customers who integrate against it. What makes it a good contract, and how do you keep your internal model from leaking into it?

expectedPoints.label: Contract thinking
expectedPoints.description: Stable shapes, consistent errors, pagination, versioning, docs — clients build on the contract.

expectedPoints.label: DTO ≠ entity
expectedPoints.description: API objects are separate from DB/domain models; internals stay private and evolvable.

expectedPoints.label: Canonical model + explicit mapping
expectedPoints.description: Partner formats normalize to one internal model; mapping is tested and versioned.

expectedPoints.label: Validation vs business rule
expectedPoints.description: Technical form errors (400) vs domain refusals (409/422) — separated handling.

questions.title: Securing an API beyond login
questions.prompt: Authentication is done. What else belongs to API security before you expose the service?

expectedPoints.label: Backend validates everything
expectedPoints.description: Frontend is bypassable; types/enums/lengths AND data-level authorization checks.

expectedPoints.label: Secrets out of Git and logs
expectedPoints.description: Secret manager / K8s Secrets; injected at runtime.

expectedPoints.label: Encryption in transit and at rest
expectedPoints.description: TLS everywhere including service-to-service; encrypted storage/backups; neither replaces authz.

expectedPoints.label: Audit logging
expectedPoints.description: Who did what, when, old/new value — distinct from debug logs.

questions.title: Webhook or polling — and what comes with it
questions.prompt: A partner wants real-time updates about renewal status changes. Compare polling and webhooks, and describe what a production webhook implementation needs.

expectedPoints.label: Polling vs webhook trade-off
expectedPoints.description: Polling: simple, wasteful. Webhook: efficient push, but retry/security/tracking become your problem.

expectedPoints.label: Production webhook checklist
expectedPoints.description: Retries with backoff, signature validation, idempotency, timeout, delivery log.

expectedPoints.label: Cursor pagination for changing lists
expectedPoints.description: Offset duplicates/skips on moving data; cursor is stable for external APIs.

expectedPoints.label: Async for slow work
expectedPoints.description: Validate, enqueue, return pending — do not block the request on document generation.
